text:
Over a decade of managing the Eurocrisis, the European Union (EU)’s emergency governance has come under considerable pressure in terms of democratic legitimacy. Research in this respect has strongly adhered to a ‘transnational crisis exploitation’ perspective, arguing that crisis politics is increasingly used as an elite strategy to buttress power asymmetries, to undermine representative democracy, and to fuel the popular Euroscepticism that it itself, arguably, helps to produce. The fact that emergency governance exists in the first place is rarely considered from the perspective of EU citizens, that is, in terms of the extent to which EU citizens actually feel powerless under emergency governance arrangements.

Abstract: The COVID-19 crisis has struck Europe in a remarkably symmetric and synchronous manner across Member States. As such, it offers an unprecedented empirical opportunity to study European citizens’ attitudes towards emergency politics. This article combines the consequences of the emergency rule with the theoretical literature on transnational cleavages to investigate which factors shape citizens’ assessments of the COVID-19 crisis management at the domestic and European level. We investigate these competing explanations in the context of 15 EU Member States using public opinion survey data on how citizens reacted to the domestic and European responses to the crisis. Our results indicate that citizens’ assessments of the management of the pandemic are predominantly determined by their attitudes towards European integration and the state of democracy. Moreover, we confirm the primacy of the transnational cleavage as the underlying and the defining factor of Euroscepticism during times of crisis. Our results suggest that citizens’ assessments of different aspects of EU governance differ and are not characterised by widespread disaffection with respect to the European Union’s management of emergencies.
explanation: The text uses high-level, generic academic phrasing and lacks specific empirical detail, a pattern commonly produced by AI-generated writing, though it could also be a human abstract.
